content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Responding to reviews earns you bookings. Doing it by hand costs you a workweek. That gap is where most hotels quietly lose.
There’s a piece of advice every hotelier has heard: respond to your reviews. It shows up in every reputation guide, every consultant deck, every conference panel. It’s correct. It’s also, in its usual form, useless — because it never accounts for what it actually costs.
So let’s do the math nobody does.
20 × 40
Write a good review response and time yourself. Not a copy-paste line — an actual reply. You read the review properly. You figure out what the guest is really upset about, or really delighted by. You choose words that sound like a person rather than a legal department. You avoid being defensive about the thing that stung. You reread it before it goes public and permanent.
That’s about 20 minutes.
Now take a normal month. 40 reviews across your platforms.
20 minutes × 40 reviews = 13+ hours.
Thirteen hours. More than a full workday and a half, every month, spent typing into review boxes. And here’s the part that decides the outcome: none of those thirteen hours have a deadline. No guest is standing at the desk. No brand rep is calling. The task is important and never urgent — which, in the economics of a working GM’s week, means it loses. Every time.
So the replies don’t get written. Not out of apathy. Out of arithmetic.
The cost of the silence
If unanswered reviews were merely untidy, this wouldn’t matter much. They’re not.
According to Cornell (Anderson), review responses pull guests straight to your listing — responding drives booking click-throughs. Read that again in operational terms: the reply isn’t a courtesy extended to a guest who has already left. It’s a lever that moves a future guest toward clicking through and booking.
Which produces a genuinely painful situation. The single activity most directly tied to demand is the one activity that reliably runs out of time. The lever exists, it works, and no one can afford to pull it.
That’s not a motivation failure. Telling a GM to “make time for reviews” is telling them to win a thirteen-hour argument against everything else on their desk, every month, forever. It’s the wrong intervention. The task doesn’t need more willpower. It needs to cost less.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

From 13 hours to 30 minutes
Drop the cost of a reply from 20 minutes to seconds, and the entire calculation inverts. Those same 40 reviews take roughly 30 minutes.
Thirty minutes is not a heroic commitment. Thirty minutes fits in a Tuesday. Thirty minutes is a task that gets done rather than deferred — and that shift, from deferred to done, is the whole ballgame.
Four things close the gap.
The AI Reply Generator writes each reply in seconds, in your voice — you read it, adjust it if you want, and post. This is where thirteen hours becomes thirty minutes.
Review Monitoring puts every platform into one queue, so the problem stops being “which sites do I still need to check?” Nothing slips through the cracks, because there are no cracks left to slip through.
The standalone Reply Generator handles the awkward cases: a review on a platform you haven’t connected, a comment somewhere unexpected. Paste the review and the rating, get an instant reply back. Even reviews outside your connected platforms get answered.
Reports close the loop by tracking your response rate and response time over any period. This matters more than it first appears. A habit you can’t measure is a habit you can’t hold anyone to. Once responding is cheap and visible, it stops being a good intention and becomes a standard — something a GM can be accountable for and an owner can verify.
The lever was always there
Nothing in this argument is exotic. Responding to reviews works — Cornell’s finding says so, and any operator who has watched a thoughtful reply defuse a bad week knows it intuitively.
The reason it doesn’t happen isn’t mystery. It’s thirteen hours.
Take the thirteen hours away, and the advice everyone has been giving you finally becomes advice you can actually follow. Responding drives revenue. Time was the only thing killing it.
